text: What does 'WWW' stand for at the start of a web address?
funFact: 💡 The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989 at CERN. It's not the same as the internet — the internet is the physical network; the Web is the system of pages and links running on top of it. The very first website is still live at info.cern.ch.

text: What does Wi-Fi actually stand for?
options: It's a made-up brand name — nothing
answer: It's a made-up brand name — nothing
funFact: 💡 'Wi-Fi' was invented by a marketing firm to make '802.11b Direct Sequence' sound catchier. The Wi-Fi Alliance later claimed it meant 'Wireless Fidelity' — but that was retrofitted. The person who coined the term has confirmed it was always just a made-up brand name.

text: Your phone still plays music in Airplane Mode. Why?
options: Stored music doesn't need wireless — Airplane Mode only disables radios
Airplane Mode only disables calls, not apps
Downloaded music bypasses the radio via a local cache
Airplane Mode reduces battery drain, improving audio output
answer: Stored music doesn't need wireless — Airplane Mode only disables radios
funFact: 💡 Airplane Mode disables cellular, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and GPS radios simultaneously. Anything stored locally — music, photos, games, maps — still works perfectly. Many people use Airplane Mode on the ground to save battery, since disabling all those radios significantly reduces power drain.

text: What does 'QR' stand for in QR code?
funFact: 💡 QR codes were invented in 1994 by Masahiro Hara at Denso Wave — a Toyota subsidiary — to track car parts on assembly lines. Despite being invented decades ago, QR codes only became mainstream globally during the COVID-19 pandemic when contactless menus replaced printed ones overnight.

text: Which company makes the iPhone?
funFact: 💡 Steve Jobs unveiled the original iPhone on January 9, 2007, calling it 'an iPod, a phone, and an internet communicator' in one. The audience laughed because it sounded impossible. It had no App Store at launch — that came a year later. The App Store now generates over $1 trillion in transactions annually.

text: Bluetooth is named after a real historical person. Who?
options: Heinrich Blutmann, a German radio engineer
It's a made-up name with no real origin
funFact: 💡 Harald Bluetooth united warring Danish tribes in the 10th century — the technology was named after him because it was meant to unite communication protocols. The Bluetooth logo is a runic monogram combining his initials H and B. It was invented in 1994 by Ericsson and now connects over 5 billion devices worldwide.

text: What does 'the Cloud' actually mean when companies say your files are stored there?
options: Your files are on remote servers in a data centre
Your files are on a satellite in orbit
Your files are split across nearby Wi-Fi routers
Your files are cached on other users' devices
answer: Your files are on remote servers in a data centre
funFact: 💡 'The cloud' is just someone else's computer — specifically, servers in large data centres you access over the internet. Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud are the three biggest. AWS alone powers Netflix, Airbnb, and millions of apps. A single AWS data centre can consume as much electricity as a small city.

text: GPS tells you exactly where you are. What does GPS stand for?
funFact: 💡 GPS was developed by the US military in the 1970s and opened to civilians in 1983 after a Korean passenger jet was shot down for straying into Soviet airspace — a navigation error. Your phone listens to at least 4 satellites simultaneously, each beaming the exact time. The tiny differences in arrival time triangulate your position to within a few metres.

text: Which web browser has the most users worldwide?
funFact: 💡 Google Chrome holds roughly 65% of the global browser market. When it launched in 2008, Internet Explorer had over 60% market share. Chrome overtook IE by 2012 through superior speed and developer tools. Google also earns revenue every time someone uses Chrome's default Google search — making it a product that monetises itself.

text: Phone batteries die faster in cold weather. Why?
options: Cold slows the chemical reactions inside lithium-ion batteries
Cold causes the battery to leak electricity into the phone's frame
Cold physically shrinks the battery reducing its capacity
Cold makes the screen use more power
answer: Cold slows the chemical reactions inside lithium-ion batteries
funFact: 💡 Lithium-ion batteries work through electrochemical reactions — cold temperatures slow the movement of lithium ions, reducing their ability to deliver power. The battery isn't losing charge permanently; it's just temporarily unable to perform. When it warms back up, the capacity often returns. That's why phones shut off in winter but recover indoors.

text: The first computer mouse was made of which material?
funFact: 💡 Douglas Engelbart invented the mouse in 1964 at Stanford Research Institute — the prototype was a small wooden box with two metal wheels. It was called a 'mouse' because the cord looked like a tail. Engelbart first showed it publicly in 1968 in what became known as 'The Mother of All Demos' — he also demonstrated video conferencing and hyperlinks, decades before they existed.

text: Which of these is NOT a social media platform?
funFact: 💡 Dropbox is a cloud file storage service, not social media. When Steve Jobs tried to acquire Dropbox, founder Drew Houston refused. At the time it seemed like a massive risk — Dropbox later went public at a $9.2 billion valuation. Jobs reportedly told Houston that Dropbox was 'a feature, not a product'. Houston proved him wrong.

text: What does 'SSD' stand for — the fast storage inside modern laptops?
funFact: 💡 Unlike hard drives which store data on spinning magnetic discs, SSDs use flash memory chips with no moving parts — making them roughly 10× faster, silent, lighter, and far more shock-resistant. Swapping an old hard drive for an SSD is the single biggest speed upgrade most computers can receive without replacing the whole machine.

text: The first computer 'bug' was a real insect. What was it?
options: A moth inside a Harvard computer relay
A beetle that chewed an IBM cable
A fly trapped in a vacuum tube at MIT
A cockroach shorting a circuit at Bell Labs
answer: A moth inside a Harvard computer relay
funFact: 💡 In 1947, Grace Hopper's team found a real moth in Relay 70 of the Harvard Mark II computer and taped it into the logbook: 'First actual case of bug being found.' That logbook is now at the Smithsonian. The word 'bug' for a technical problem predates this — engineers used it informally for decades — but this incident immortalised it.

text: You tap your phone on a payment terminal with no PIN. What technology makes this work?
funFact: 💡 NFC works at under 4 cm — which is why you have to physically tap. It uses electromagnetic induction to transfer a small payment token. Apple Pay, Google Pay, and contactless bank cards all use NFC. It also powers hotel key cards, transit passes, and the tap-to-pair feature on Bluetooth headphones.

text: What does AI stand for?
funFact: 💡 The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956 at a Dartmouth conference. Modern AI systems like large language models are trained on hundreds of billions of words and can generate text, code, and images. McCarthy probably didn't envision AI writing university essays when he first defined the field.

text: Your password gets 'hashed' before a website stores it. What does that mean?
options: Converted to a scrambled code that cannot be reversed
Encrypted and stored with a matching decryption key
Split across multiple servers for security
Compressed to save storage space on the database
answer: Converted to a scrambled code that cannot be reversed
funFact: 💡 A hashing function converts your password into a fixed-length code — even a one-character change produces a completely different hash. When you log in, the site hashes what you typed and compares it to the stored hash. The original password is never stored anywhere. When sites report a 'breach', they usually mean hashes were stolen — not plain-text passwords.

text: Ctrl+Z undoes your last action. Where was this shortcut invented?
options: Xerox PARC in the 1970s — same lab that invented the mouse
Microsoft Word in 1989 when undo was first added to software
IBM who standardised it across all keyboards
answer: Xerox PARC in the 1970s — same lab that invented the mouse
funFact: 💡 Xerox PARC was arguably the most influential technology lab in history — it invented the graphical user interface, the computer mouse, Ethernet networking, the laser printer, and the concept of undo. Steve Jobs visited PARC in 1979 and used what he saw to build the Macintosh. Xerox never fully commercialised any of it.

text: Which company makes the Android operating system used on most smartphones worldwide?
funFact: 💡 Android was created by a startup that Google acquired in 2005 for $50 million. It launched publicly in 2008 and now runs on roughly 72% of all smartphones globally — over 3 billion active devices. Samsung, Sony, Xiaomi, and hundreds of manufacturers all build on top of Google's Android platform.

text: Your phone has 128 GB of storage. What does 'GB' mean?
options: Gigabyte — roughly one billion bytes of data
Gigabit — a measure of download speed
Graphics Buffer — memory reserved for the screen
General Bandwidth — total data the phone can handle
answer: Gigabyte — roughly one billion bytes of data
funFact: 💡 The original IBM PC in 1981 had 64 kilobytes of RAM — 0.000064 GB. A single smartphone photo today is 4–8 MB. A two-hour HD movie is 4–8 GB. The entire printed text of the internet would be hundreds of exabytes — one exabyte equals one billion gigabytes. Storage has grown by a factor of roughly a million in 40 years.

text: What is 'phishing' in cybersecurity?
options: Tricking someone into revealing passwords by pretending to be trusted
Hacking a website by flooding it with traffic
Installing hidden software to monitor a device
Guessing passwords by trying millions of combinations
answer: Tricking someone into revealing passwords by pretending to be trusted
funFact: 💡 'Phishing' uses 'ph' instead of 'f' — a reference to 1990s hacker slang. Phishing emails account for over 90% of all successful data breaches. The most sophisticated attacks impersonate a real colleague or manager using genuine email thread history — called 'spear phishing'. The most secure defence is simply pausing before clicking any link.

text: 5G is the latest mobile network. What does the '5' refer to?
funFact: 💡 The 'G' stands for generation: 1G (1980s, analogue voice), 2G (1990s, SMS), 3G (2000s, mobile internet), 4G (2010s, HD streaming), 5G (2020s, ultra-low latency). 5G is engineered primarily for connected vehicles and IoT devices as much as for faster phone speeds.

text: Face ID and fingerprint unlock use what type of security?
funFact: 💡 Your iPhone's Face ID maps over 30,000 infrared dots onto your face to build a 3D model. It's accurate to 1 in 1,000,000 — compared to 1 in 50,000 for a fingerprint sensor. It still works in complete darkness because it uses infrared, not visible light. The face map adapts over time as your appearance changes.

text: What does 'streaming' a video actually mean — how does it reach your screen?
options: The video is sent in small chunks continuously as you watch
The full video downloads before playback begins
The video is broadcast live from the studio each time you press play
The video is reconstructed from pieces on nearby devices
answer: The video is sent in small chunks continuously as you watch
funFact: 💡 Streaming sends video in small segments — typically 2–10 seconds at a time — buffered slightly ahead of playback. Netflix dynamically adjusts video quality dozens of times per minute based on your connection speed. That's why quality drops during slow periods and recovers automatically — the app is constantly adapting to your bandwidth.

text: Who is widely considered the first computer programmer in history?
funFact: 💡 Ada Lovelace wrote what is recognised as the first algorithm intended for a machine — Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine — in 1843, before computers existed. She was the daughter of the poet Lord Byron. The US Department of Defense later named a programming language 'Ada' in her honour. She died at just 36 years old.

text: The first webcam was invented to do something surprisingly mundane. What was it?
options: Watch a coffee pot at Cambridge University
Monitor a car park at MIT
Check weather equipment on a rooftop
Observe a printer queue at Xerox PARC
answer: Watch a coffee pot at Cambridge University
funFact: 💡 In 1991, researchers at Cambridge's computer lab were fed up walking to the coffee room only to find the pot empty. They set up a camera pointed at the pot and wrote software to stream the image to their computers. It became the world's first webcam and eventually went live on the internet, attracting millions of curious viewers worldwide before being switched off in 2001.

text: Your phone has 4 GB of RAM and 128 GB of storage. What's the difference?
options: RAM is working memory; storage holds files permanently
RAM stores photos; storage runs the operating system
RAM is faster storage; 128 GB is for streaming only
There is no real difference — both store the same types of data
answer: RAM is working memory; storage holds files permanently
funFact: 💡 RAM (Random Access Memory) is your phone's short-term memory — it holds whatever apps are currently running. When you close an app, it leaves RAM. Storage is long-term — your photos, apps, and music stay there even when the phone is off. More RAM means more apps can stay open simultaneously without needing to reload.

text: Two-factor authentication (2FA) sends a code to your phone. What threat does it actually protect against?
options: Someone who has your password but not your phone
Malware already installed on your device
Your internet provider spying on your account
answer: Someone who has your password but not your phone
funFact: 💡 2FA works on the principle of 'something you know' (password) plus 'something you have' (your phone). Even if a hacker steals your password in a data breach, they still can't log in without the second code. Security experts say enabling 2FA on your email is the single most impactful thing most people can do to protect their digital life.

text: Dark mode on your phone is said to save battery. When is this actually true?
options: Only on OLED screens — pixels turn off completely for black areas
On all screens, because dark colours use less processing power
Never — dark mode only reduces eye strain, not battery use
Only when the screen brightness is above 50%
answer: Only on OLED screens — pixels turn off completely for black areas
funFact: 💡 OLED (Organic LED) screens light each pixel individually — displaying true black means those pixels are physically off, using zero power. LCD screens have a permanent backlight that stays on regardless of colour, so dark mode saves almost nothing on LCD displays. This is why Apple specifically highlighted OLED when promoting Dark Mode battery benefits.

text: Google processes roughly how many search queries every single day?
funFact: 💡 Google handles an estimated 8.5 billion searches per day — about 99,000 every second. Despite this, Google's search index only covers a fraction of the actual internet. The vast majority of online content sits in the 'deep web', accessible only through direct links or login — and is never indexed by any search engine.

text: What does 'https' at the start of a web address mean compared to 'http'?
options: The connection is encrypted — data is scrambled in transit
The website has been verified as trustworthy by Google
The page loads faster using a compressed protocol
The website stores no personal data about you
answer: The connection is encrypted — data is scrambled in transit
funFact: 💡 HTTPS uses TLS (Transport Layer Security) to encrypt data between your browser and the server. Without it, anyone on the same Wi-Fi network could intercept your login details using a packet sniffer — a common attack in cafés and airports. The padlock icon in your browser confirms an encrypted connection is active.

text: Moore's Law predicted computer chips would double in power roughly every two years. Who was Moore?
options: Alan Moore, a Bell Labs engineer
Gary Moore, IBM's chief chip designer
Moore was a fictional character in a 1965 paper
funFact: 💡 Gordon Moore made this observation in a 1965 paper and it held remarkably true for over 50 years — driving exponential improvements in computing power while costs fell. Modern chips are now so dense that their transistors are just a few atoms wide, and many engineers believe Moore's Law is finally approaching its physical limit.

text: Which of these social media platforms was acquired by Microsoft?
funFact: 💡 Microsoft acquired LinkedIn in 2016 for $26.2 billion — one of the largest tech acquisitions ever. LinkedIn has over 1 billion members and is particularly dominant in professional networking. Despite being owned by Microsoft, it continues to operate independently with its own CEO and brand.

text: What does a VPN actually do to protect your internet connection?
options: Encrypts your traffic and hides your IP address from websites
Speeds up your internet by routing through faster servers
Blocks ads and prevents tracking across websites
Prevents your internet provider from charging data overages
answer: Encrypts your traffic and hides your IP address from websites
funFact: 💡 A VPN creates an encrypted tunnel between your device and a server elsewhere — websites see the VPN server's IP address, not yours. This prevents your internet provider from seeing what sites you visit. However, your trust shifts from your ISP to the VPN provider — who can technically see all your traffic instead.

text: The first iPhone had no App Store. How did Apple add new features before it launched?
options: Web apps accessed through Safari — no native apps at all
A limited app store invitation-only for developers
Features were added via monthly software updates only
Physical accessories plugged into the dock connector
answer: Web apps accessed through Safari — no native apps at all
funFact: 💡 When Steve Jobs unveiled the iPhone in January 2007, he told developers to build web apps — accessed through the browser — not native apps. The App Store didn't launch until July 2008. Within 60 days of opening, it had 60 million downloads. Today it hosts over 1.8 million apps.

text: Which company invented the touchscreen technology used in modern smartphones?
options: It was developed by multiple researchers across decades, not one company
Apple in 2007 with the first iPhone
Samsung in 2003 for early touch PDAs
Microsoft in the 1990s for tablet PCs
answer: It was developed by multiple researchers across decades, not one company
funFact: 💡 Capacitive touch technology was first described academically in 1965. Fingerworks (acquired by Apple in 2005) developed multi-touch gestures. Apple combined these with a phone, polished the interface, and made it mainstream. The 'pinch to zoom' gesture that feels natural today required years of engineering to work reliably at human-finger scale.

text: What does 'open source' software mean?
options: The source code is publicly available for anyone to view and modify
The software is free to download and use commercially
The software has no security vulnerabilities
The code is written by volunteer developers only
answer: The source code is publicly available for anyone to view and modify
funFact: 💡 Linux (powers most of the internet), Android, Firefox, and VS Code are all open source. The Linux kernel alone has contributions from over 15,000 developers and is maintained by major tech companies including Google, Microsoft, and Meta — all contributing code to software that runs their competitors' systems.

text: Incognito mode keeps your browsing private. Which of these statements is TRUE about it?
options: Your internet provider and websites can still see your activity
It hides your location from all websites
It prevents all tracking including from your device's apps
It deletes your browsing history from your internet provider's servers
answer: Your internet provider and websites can still see your activity
funFact: 💡 Incognito mode only prevents the browser from saving your history, cookies, and form data locally on your device. Your internet provider, your employer's network, and the websites you visit can all still see exactly what you're doing. In 2024, Google settled a $5 billion lawsuit over misleading users about Incognito's privacy.

text: Which company owns Instagram, WhatsApp, and Facebook?
funFact: 💡 Mark Zuckerberg rebranded Facebook to Meta in 2021, announcing a pivot toward the 'metaverse'. Meta owns Facebook ($1B acquisition value, 2004), Instagram (acquired 2012 for $1B), and WhatsApp (acquired 2014 for $19B). At the time, paying $19 billion for a free messaging app seemed absurd — WhatsApp now has over 2 billion users.

text: Why does your Wi-Fi router have both a 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz band?
options: 5 GHz is faster but shorter range; 2.4 GHz is slower but reaches further
2.4 GHz is for phones; 5 GHz is reserved for laptops only
5 GHz has no interference; 2.4 GHz is for outdoor use
They're identical — the dual band is only for marketing
answer: 5 GHz is faster but shorter range; 2.4 GHz is slower but reaches further
funFact: 💡 Higher frequency radio waves carry more data but are absorbed more easily by walls, furniture, and even the water in your body. 2.4 GHz penetrates obstacles better and is why older devices default to it. Microwaves also operate at 2.4 GHz — which is why a microwave oven can disrupt nearby Wi-Fi signals.

text: What is an algorithm — in plain terms?
options: A step-by-step set of instructions to solve a problem
A mathematical formula used only in statistics
A type of encrypted code used in cybersecurity
A programming language developed at MIT
answer: A step-by-step set of instructions to solve a problem
funFact: 💡 Every time TikTok decides what video to show you next, every time Google ranks a search result, and every time Spotify generates a playlist — an algorithm is making those decisions. The word 'algorithm' comes from the name of 9th-century Persian mathematician Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi, whose work introduced algebra to the Western world.

text: Which tech company has the highest market value in history as of the early 2020s?
funFact: 💡 Apple became the first US company to reach a $1 trillion market cap in 2018, then $2 trillion in 2020, and $3 trillion in 2022. The iPhone generates roughly 50% of Apple's total revenue. In 1997, Apple was weeks from bankruptcy before Steve Jobs returned and turned it around — one of the greatest corporate comebacks in history.

text: Emails are never truly deleted from the internet. Which statement is TRUE?
options: Sent emails are copies on both the sender's and recipient's servers
Deleted emails are wiped from all servers within 24 hours
Email providers are legally required to delete emails after 30 days
Emails are stored only on your device, not on any server
answer: Sent emails are copies on both the sender's and recipient's servers
funFact: 💡 When you send an email, copies exist on your outbox server, in transit servers, and on the recipient's inbox server — and potentially their backup servers too. Deleting it from your inbox removes your copy but not theirs. Many companies retain emails for years for legal compliance. In major lawsuits, years-old emails are regularly subpoenaed as evidence.

text: What does 'pixel' mean — literally?
options: Pixel is a brand name with no literal meaning
funFact: 💡 'Pixel' is a portmanteau of 'picture' and 'element' — first used in the 1960s in space imaging. A 4K screen has 3,840 × 2,160 = over 8 million pixels. Each pixel is controlled individually and can display any of over 16 million colours. The human eye can only distinguish individual pixels below about a 300 PPI (pixels per inch) density.

text: Cookies track you across the web. What exactly IS a web cookie?
options: A small text file stored on your device by a website
A hidden image pixel that records page visits
A type of encrypted script that runs in the background
A cached copy of the webpage stored for faster loading
answer: A small text file stored on your device by a website
funFact: 💡 Web cookies were invented in 1994 by Netscape engineer Lou Montulli to solve a simple problem: keeping users logged in. He named them after 'magic cookies' in Unix computing. Third-party cookies track you across different websites — that's why you search for shoes and then see shoe ads everywhere. Major browsers are phasing them out.

text: What does 'USB' stand for — the port on almost every device?
funFact: 💡 USB was developed in 1996 by a consortium of companies including Intel, Microsoft, and IBM — specifically to replace the nightmare of incompatible ports (serial, parallel, PS/2, SCSI) on early PCs. Before USB, plugging in a keyboard or mouse often required restarting the computer. The 'universal' in the name was the goal: one standard for everything.

text: How does Face ID know it's really you — not just a photo of you?
options: It projects 30,000 infrared dots to create a 3D depth map of your face
It checks the reflection in your eyes to verify you're looking at a screen
It analyses micro-expressions that change when you're not in person
It combines face recognition with your device's gyroscope data
answer: It projects 30,000 infrared dots to create a 3D depth map of your face
funFact: 💡 Face ID uses a dot projector, infrared camera, and flood illuminator to map your face in three dimensions. A flat photo has no depth and fails the check. The system also uses a neural engine to detect liveness — blinking, slight movements — and adapts to gradual changes like glasses, beard growth, or ageing. Apple claims a 1-in-1,000,000 false acceptance rate.

text: What makes an SSD faster than a traditional hard drive for everyday tasks?
options: No moving parts — data is read electronically not mechanically
SSDs run at higher voltage, processing requests faster
SSDs have a dedicated processor that prioritises common tasks
SSDs store data in a single layer, reducing search time
answer: No moving parts — data is read electronically not mechanically
funFact: 💡 A traditional hard drive has a physical spinning disc and a read head that physically moves to find data — like a record player. This mechanical process takes milliseconds. An SSD reads data by sending electrical signals through flash memory chips — almost instantaneous. The difference is like comparing flipping a light switch to physically walking to the lamp.

text: TikTok's algorithm is considered one of the most powerful recommendation systems ever built. What key advantage does it have?
options: It starts making accurate predictions after just a few seconds of watch behaviour
It analyses the audio of every video to match your personality
It cross-references your contacts to predict what your friends like
It requires you to rate videos before personalising your feed
answer: It starts making accurate predictions after just a few seconds of watch behaviour
funFact: 💡 TikTok's 'For You Page' algorithm uses watch time, replays, pauses, and scroll speed — not just likes and follows — to build a model of your preferences. It can identify your interests faster than you consciously recognise them yourself. This is why new users are hooked within minutes: the algorithm personalises aggressively from the very first video.

text: What is a 'deepfake'?
options: AI-generated video or audio that realistically mimics a real person
A fake online identity used to manipulate social media
A phishing attack disguised as a trusted website
Digitally altered statistics in data journalism
answer: AI-generated video or audio that realistically mimics a real person
funFact: 💡 Deepfake technology uses deep learning models (specifically GANs — Generative Adversarial Networks) trained on thousands of images of a person. The name combines 'deep learning' and 'fake'. Early deepfakes required huge datasets and powerful computers; by 2024, convincing deepfakes could be generated from a single photo in seconds on a standard laptop.

text: Incognito mode hides you from your internet provider. True or false?
options: False — your ISP still sees everything
True — but only on mobile networks
answer: False — your ISP still sees everything
funFact: 💡 Incognito only stops your browser saving history locally. Your internet provider, employer's network, and every website you visit can all still see your activity. In 2024 Google settled a $5 billion lawsuit over misleading users about exactly this.

text: Which one sounds fake but is actually true about AI image generators?
options: They struggle with human hands — often generating extra or missing fingers
They can't produce images with text in them
They require a 3D model as input to generate realistic people
They only work with photographs, not drawings
answer: They struggle with human hands — often generating extra or missing fingers
funFact: 💡 AI image models like Midjourney and DALL-E famously botch hands — producing six fingers, fused knuckles, or twisted anatomy. Hands are complex, varied in photos, and hard for models trained on image patterns to reconstruct correctly. It's now the easiest way to spot an AI-generated image.

text: Which happened FIRST in tech history?
options: The first text message was sent
answer: The first text message was sent
funFact: 💡 The first SMS was sent on December 3, 1992 — it said 'Merry Christmas'. The first website went live in 1991 (CERN). Google was founded in 1998. The iPhone launched in 2007. Texting is older than Google, yet it's still one of the most widely used communication tools on Earth.

text: Your Wi-Fi router has a 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz band. Which goes through walls better?
options: 2.4 GHz — lower frequency penetrates obstacles more easily
5 GHz — higher frequency has more power
They're identical — walls don't affect either
5 GHz — it switches automatically to avoid walls
answer: 2.4 GHz — lower frequency penetrates obstacles more easily
funFact: 💡 Higher radio frequencies carry more data but are absorbed by walls, furniture, and even the water in your body. That's why 5 GHz is blazing fast in the same room but drops off sharply through walls. Microwaves also run at 2.4 GHz — which is why a microwave running in the kitchen can interfere with nearby Wi-Fi.

text: Which statement about ChatGPT is TRUE?
options: It predicts the most likely next word — it doesn't truly 'understand' anything
It connects to the internet to look up answers in real time
It remembers every conversation across all users to improve responses
It reads your question in your actual language without any translation
answer: It predicts the most likely next word — it doesn't truly 'understand' anything
funFact: 💡 Large language models like GPT work by predicting the statistically most likely next token based on patterns in billions of training examples. There's no comprehension — just extremely sophisticated pattern matching. This is why AI can sound confident and completely wrong at the same time.

text: A password manager stores all your passwords. Which option correctly describes how it protects them?
options: They're encrypted — only your master password can decrypt them
They're hidden in a private cloud visible only to you
They're split across devices so no single server holds your data
They're saved as images so they can't be read as text
answer: They're encrypted — only your master password can decrypt them
funFact: 💡 Password managers use end-to-end encryption — your passwords are scrambled before leaving your device. The company itself cannot read them. If you forget your master password, even the provider cannot recover your vault. Security experts universally recommend password managers over reusing passwords.

text: Which statement about electric vehicle batteries is FALSE?
options: EV batteries are made from lithium so they can be fully recycled without waste
Cold weather significantly reduces an EV's driving range
EV batteries degrade slowly over time, losing maximum capacity
Fast charging repeatedly can accelerate battery degradation
answer: EV batteries are made from lithium so they can be fully recycled without waste
funFact: 💡 EV battery recycling is one of the biggest unsolved challenges in green technology. Lithium-ion batteries contain cobalt, nickel, and manganese — all difficult to recover cleanly. As of 2024, less than 5% of lithium-ion batteries are recycled effectively, making end-of-life battery disposal a growing environmental concern.

text: Cookies on websites are used for tracking. Which one is NOT what cookies do?
options: Speed up your internet connection by compressing web traffic
Keep you logged in after closing and reopening a browser
Remember your shopping cart items between sessions
Track which pages you visit for advertising purposes
answer: Speed up your internet connection by compressing web traffic
funFact: 💡 Cookies are small text files — not programs. They store data like your session token (so you stay logged in), preferences, and browsing behaviour. 'Third-party cookies' placed by ad networks track you across different websites, building detailed profiles. That's what cookie consent banners are actually about.

text: You get a message: 'Your iCloud is full. Click here to upgrade.' Which is the most reliable way to tell if it's a scam?
options: Check the actual sender email address — scam domains never match Apple's
See if the message has a logo — real Apple messages always include one
Call the number in the message to verify
Check if the link starts with https
answer: Check the actual sender email address — scam domains never match Apple's
funFact: 💡 Phishing emails impersonate Apple, banks, and delivery companies by copying their exact design. But they can't fake the sending domain — look for addresses like 'support@apple-secure-id.com' instead of '@apple.com'. A padlock and https only mean the connection is encrypted — they say nothing about whether the site is legitimate.

text: Which correctly explains why SSD laptops boot so much faster than HDD laptops?
options: SSDs have no moving parts — data is read instantly from memory chips
SSDs run at a higher voltage, powering the CPU faster at startup
SSDs cache the operating system in RAM before you press the power button
SSDs use a compressed version of Windows that loads in fewer steps
answer: SSDs have no moving parts — data is read instantly from memory chips
funFact: 💡 Hard drives spin a magnetic disc at 5,400–7,200 RPM and move a physical read arm to find data — that mechanical delay adds seconds every time. SSDs access any data in microseconds with no movement at all. Swapping a hard drive for an SSD is still the single biggest speed upgrade most computers can receive.

text: Which of these is the correct explanation for why 5G can be faster than 4G?
options: It uses higher frequency waves that carry more data — but they travel shorter distances
It routes traffic through satellites instead of ground towers
It uses less compression so video quality is naturally higher
It doubles the number of SIM cards a phone can hold simultaneously
answer: It uses higher frequency waves that carry more data — but they travel shorter distances
funFact: 💡 5G uses millimetre-wave spectrum at up to 100 GHz — compared to 4G's 0.7–2.6 GHz. These waves carry enormous bandwidth but are blocked by buildings, rain, and even leaves. That's why 5G towers need to be placed every few hundred metres in cities rather than kilometres apart.

text: Which of these is a myth about smartphone cameras?
options: More megapixels always means a better photo
Night mode uses multiple exposures to brighten low-light shots
Portrait mode uses software to blur the background, not a lens
Optical zoom preserves image quality better than digital zoom
answer: More megapixels always means a better photo
funFact: 💡 Megapixels measure resolution — how many pixels are in the image — not quality. A 200MP sensor on a tiny phone chip captures smaller, noisier pixels than a 12MP professional camera with a large sensor. Sensor size, aperture, and computational photography matter far more than megapixel count.

text: True wireless earbuds like AirPods use Bluetooth. Which limitation does Bluetooth have that Wi-Fi doesn't?
options: Bluetooth is designed for short range, low power personal devices — not fast data transfer
Bluetooth requires a SIM card to function without a phone nearby
Bluetooth only works on Apple and Google devices, not cross-platform
Bluetooth requires line of sight — it can't pass through your body
answer: Bluetooth is designed for short range, low power personal devices — not fast data transfer
funFact: 💡 Bluetooth was intentionally designed for low-power, short-range connections — earbuds, keyboards, heart rate monitors. It uses just a fraction of the power Wi-Fi needs. Audio quality is limited not by hardware but by Bluetooth's audio codecs — which is why audiophiles still prefer wired headphones for high-fidelity listening.

text: Which correctly explains what happens when you 'delete' a file on your phone or computer?
options: The space is marked as available — the data stays until overwritten
The file is permanently wiped from the storage chip immediately
The file is compressed and stored in a hidden recovery folder
The file's name is removed but the content moves to RAM temporarily
answer: The space is marked as available — the data stays until overwritten
funFact: 💡 Deleting a file only removes its reference in the file system. The actual data remains on the drive until new data overwrites it. This is why forensic software can recover 'deleted' files from years ago — and why securely erasing sensitive data requires overwriting it, not just deleting it.

text: Which of these statements about GPS is TRUE?
options: GPS only receives signals — your phone never transmits your location to satellites
GPS satellites track your exact location and send it to governments
GPS requires a cellular connection to calculate your position
GPS becomes more accurate in cities because more satellites are visible
answer: GPS only receives signals — your phone never transmits your location to satellites
funFact: 💡 GPS is purely a receiving system — your device listens to signals from at least 4 satellites and calculates its own position from the tiny differences in signal arrival times. Nothing is sent back to the satellites. If apps know your location, it's because they requested it from your phone's GPS chip — not because satellites are tracking you.

text: Which one does NOT belong with the others?
funFact: 💡 JPEG, PNG, and GIF are all image file formats — MP4 is a video container format. GIFs can be animated but store each frame as a static image. Ironically, GIF is now mostly used for short looping clips that could be far smaller as MP4 — but GIFs became a cultural format and stuck around despite the inefficiency.

text: A smartwatch tracks your heart rate using light on your wrist. How does that actually work?
options: Green LEDs shine into your skin — blood absorbs light differently when pumping
Infrared sensors measure heat pulses generated by each heartbeat
Tiny electrodes detect electrical signals from your heart through your wrist
Pressure sensors in the band detect pulse vibrations in the artery
answer: Green LEDs shine into your skin — blood absorbs light differently when pumping
funFact: 💡 This is called photoplethysmography (PPG). Green light is absorbed by haemoglobin in blood — the sensor measures how much light reflects back, which varies with each heartbeat as blood volume in your wrist changes. The same technique is used in hospital pulse oximeters, but those use red and infrared light to also measure blood oxygen.

text: Which explanation correctly describes what end-to-end encryption (E2E) actually means in WhatsApp?
options: Only the sender and recipient can read messages — not WhatsApp itself
Messages are encrypted on WhatsApp's servers but staff can access them with a warrant
Encryption applies to messages in transit but they're stored unencrypted on the server
End-to-end means the message is encrypted only between your phone and the nearest Wi-Fi router
answer: Only the sender and recipient can read messages — not WhatsApp itself
funFact: 💡 WhatsApp uses the Signal Protocol for E2E encryption. Messages are encrypted on your device and can only be decrypted by the recipient's device. WhatsApp's servers only ever see scrambled data — they cannot read your messages even if legally compelled to hand them over. This is why law enforcement agencies have repeatedly clashed with encrypted messaging apps.

text: Which of these correctly describes how contactless payments stay secure?
options: A one-time token is generated per transaction — your real card number is never transmitted
Your PIN is encrypted and sent with each tap for verification
The terminal stores your card number locally for fraud checks
NFC signals are too short-range for anyone to intercept
answer: A one-time token is generated per transaction — your real card number is never transmitted
funFact: 💡 Tokenisation means your actual card number never leaves your phone or bank. Each tap generates a unique, single-use code — even if someone intercepted the signal, the code would be useless for any other transaction. This is actually more secure than swiping a physical card, which transmits your real card number every time.

text: Which of these is an actual cybersecurity attack type — not a made-up one?
funFact: 💡 A man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack intercepts communication between two parties without either knowing. Common on unsecured public Wi-Fi — an attacker positions their device between you and the router, reading all your traffic. HTTPS encryption prevents most MITM attacks, which is one reason the padlock in your browser actually matters.

text: Fast charging can damage your battery long-term. Which answer is most accurate?
options: Frequent fast charging does accelerate battery degradation over time
Fast charging has no effect — batteries degrade at the same rate regardless
Fast charging only damages batteries if used more than once a day
Fast charging is harmful only if the charger isn't from the original manufacturer
answer: Frequent fast charging does accelerate battery degradation over time
funFact: 💡 Fast charging pushes more current into the battery in a short time, generating heat — and heat is a lithium-ion battery's biggest enemy. Most phones slow charging automatically when the battery is above 80% to limit heat damage. Apple's Optimised Battery Charging feature learns your routine and deliberately slows charging overnight to preserve long-term battery health.

text: Which correctly describes how a deepfake video is made?
options: AI analyses thousands of images of a person and synthesises their face onto different footage
Real actors wear motion capture suits and their appearance is digitally replaced
Deepfakes are composited frame-by-frame using video editing software
A 3D scan of the target person is required before any deepfake can be generated
answer: AI analyses thousands of images of a person and synthesises their face onto different footage
funFact: 💡 Deepfakes use GANs — Generative Adversarial Networks — where two AI systems compete: one generates fake faces, the other tries to detect them. By 2024, convincing deepfakes can be made from a single photo in seconds on a regular laptop. Audio deepfakes are even easier to produce and are already being used in phone scams impersonating family members.

text: USB-C and USB-A both charge devices. Which statement about USB-C is TRUE?
options: USB-C is reversible — it works either way up, unlike USB-A
USB-C delivers faster charging because it uses a different voltage standard than USB-A
USB-C is exclusive to Apple devices — Android phones use a different version
USB-C cables carry data only — a separate power cable is still required for charging
answer: USB-C is reversible — it works either way up, unlike USB-A
funFact: 💡 USB-A has been frustrating users since 1996 — it only inserts one way, but it's nearly impossible to tell which way is correct without looking. USB-C fixed this with a symmetrical connector that works in either orientation. USB-C also supports USB 3.2 and Thunderbolt 4, enabling data transfers of up to 40 Gbps — fast enough to move an entire HD movie in under a second.

text: Smart TVs can be hacked. Which of these is a real security risk they introduce?
options: Built-in microphones and cameras can be accessed if the TV has unpatched software
Smart TVs broadcast your Wi-Fi password to nearby devices by default
The TV's HDMI ports can transmit malware from connected consoles
Smart TVs share viewing data with your ISP unless manually disabled on the router
answer: Built-in microphones and cameras can be accessed if the TV has unpatched software
funFact: 💡 In 2019, Samsung warned customers that their smart TVs should be regularly virus-scanned. The CIA's Vault 7 documents (leaked in 2017) revealed a hacking tool called 'Weeping Angel' that could turn certain Samsung TVs into listening devices — leaving them appearing off while secretly recording audio. Keeping smart device firmware updated is the primary defence.

text: Which correctly explains why your streaming quality drops during peak evening hours?
options: More people are online simultaneously — ISPs throttle bandwidth when networks are congested
Streaming servers automatically reduce quality at night to save energy costs
Your router overheats from continuous use throughout the day
Satellites carrying streaming data move further away during the evening
answer: More people are online simultaneously — ISPs throttle bandwidth when networks are congested
funFact: 💡 In the UK, 8–10pm is called 'the internet rush hour' — the same network infrastructure used by offices during the day gets hammered by millions of households streaming simultaneously. Netflix and YouTube both compress and reduce stream quality automatically when they detect congestion. Netflix alone accounts for roughly 15% of all global internet traffic during peak hours.

text: Which planet is known as the Red Planet?
funFact: 💡 Mars looks red because its surface is coated in iron oxide — basically rust. Its dust storms can grow so large they swallow the entire planet for months at a time.

text: What is the closest star to Earth?
funFact: 💡 The Sun is only 8 light-minutes away. The next nearest star, Proxima Centauri, is so far that its light takes over 4 years to reach us — and that's our closest stellar neighbour.

text: Which animal is the largest on Earth?
funFact: 💡 A blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and weighs about 180 kg. Its heartbeat can be picked up by sensors over 3 km away, and a human could crawl through its largest arteries.

text: What force pulls everything toward the ground?
funFact: 💡 Gravity doesn't just work on Earth — it's the force holding the entire universe together. It keeps the Moon orbiting Earth, Earth orbiting the Sun, and galaxies from flying apart.

text: What gas do humans breathe out?
funFact: 💡 You exhale about 200 ml of carbon dioxide every minute. Plants absorb it and convert it into food and oxygen — so in a very real sense, trees are breathing what you breathe out.

text: What is lightning?
funFact: 💡 A single lightning bolt is about 5 times hotter than the surface of the Sun — reaching 30,000°C. It lasts less than a second but could power a light bulb for around three months.

text: How many bones does an adult human body have?
funFact: 💡 Babies are born with around 270 bones. As they grow, many fuse together — a process that continues until around age 25, when the adult count of 206 is finally complete.

text: Which planet has a giant storm bigger than Earth?
funFact: 💡 Jupiter's Great Red Spot is a storm that has been raging for at least 350 years. It's larger than our entire planet — though scientists have noticed it slowly shrinking over the past few decades.

text: What do plants produce during photosynthesis?
funFact: 💡 Every single breath of oxygen you've ever taken was produced by a plant or ocean phytoplankton through photosynthesis. The Amazon alone produces enough oxygen for about 20% of the world's needs.

text: What is the hardest natural material on Earth?
funFact: 💡 Diamonds form about 150 km underground under crushing pressure and extreme heat. They're made of pure carbon — the same element as pencil graphite, just arranged very differently.

text: What is the most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere?
funFact: 💡 About 78% of every breath you take is nitrogen — a gas your body doesn't actually use. Oxygen is only 21%. Yet nitrogen is essential for life; it's a building block of proteins and DNA.

text: Which planet has beautiful rings around it?
funFact: 💡 Saturn's rings are made of billions of ice and rock chunks ranging from tiny grains to house-sized boulders. Despite stretching 282,000 km wide, they're sometimes only 10 metres thick — like a sheet of paper scaled up to a continent.

text: Which planet is the hottest in our solar system?
funFact: 💡 Venus is hotter than Mercury even though Mercury is closer to the Sun. Venus has a thick CO₂ atmosphere that traps heat — the greenhouse effect on steroids, pushing surface temperatures to 465°C. Hot enough to melt lead.

text: What is the main source of energy for life on Earth?
funFact: 💡 The Sun releases more energy in one second than humanity has used in its entire history. Almost all energy on Earth — food, fossil fuels, wind, and weather — can ultimately be traced back to sunlight.

text: What is the chemical formula for water?
funFact: 💡 Water is one of the only substances that expands when it freezes. That's why ice floats and why pipes burst in winter — frozen water takes up about 9% more space than liquid water.

text: What do we call the layer of gases surrounding Earth?
funFact: 💡 Earth's atmosphere is about 100 km thick but 75% is crammed into the bottom 11 km. Without it, there'd be no weather, no breathable air, and temperatures would swing wildly between +120°C and -170°C every single day.

text: What is the powerhouse of the cell?
funFact: 💡 Mitochondria have their own DNA — evidence they were once free-living bacteria absorbed into larger cells billions of years ago and never left. They produce the energy that powers everything your body does.

text: Which dinosaur had an extremely long neck and tiny head?
funFact: 💡 Brachiosaurus could reach leaves 9 metres off the ground — as tall as a three-storey building. It likely weighed up to 60 tonnes and needed to eat hundreds of kilograms of plants every single day just to survive.

text: What type of animal is a dolphin?
funFact: 💡 Dolphins breathe through a blowhole and nurse their young with milk — classic mammal traits. They also sleep with one half of their brain at a time, keeping the other half awake so they never stop breathing.

text: Why is the sky blue?
funFact: 💡 This is called Rayleigh scattering. Blue light has a shorter wavelength so it bounces around in the atmosphere more. At sunset, light travels through more atmosphere, the blue scatters away, and warm reds and oranges take over.

text: What does your brain use to send messages around your body?
funFact: 💡 Your brain generates enough electricity to power a small light bulb. Nerve signals travel at up to 120 metres per second — that's why you feel pain almost instantly when you stub a toe.

text: What do you call a scientist who studies volcanoes?
funFact: 💡 There are about 1,500 potentially active volcanoes on Earth and 50–70 erupt every year. Billions of years ago, volcanic eruptions were Earth's primary source of atmosphere and water vapour — they literally built the air we breathe.

text: What is the freezing point of water?
funFact: 💡 32°C is the freezing point in Fahrenheit — a classic mix-up. The Celsius scale was designed to be logical: 0° for freezing, 100° for boiling. It's used by almost every country in the world except the USA.

text: Which organ pumps blood around your body?
funFact: 💡 Your heart beats about 100,000 times a day without you doing anything. Over a lifetime that's more than 2.5 billion beats — and it never takes a day off from the moment it starts in the womb.

text: How do plants make their own food?
funFact: 💡 Plants take in CO₂ and water, use sunlight as energy, and produce glucose and oxygen as a result. Every breath of oxygen you've ever taken was made by this process — you literally owe your life to it.

text: The Sun is a giant ball of fire. True or false?
options: True — but it's plasma, not gas
funFact: 💡 Fire requires oxygen, and space has none. The Sun produces energy by fusing hydrogen atoms into helium under extreme pressure. This process releases a million times more energy per kilogram than burning coal.

text: Which planet has the most confirmed moons?
funFact: 💡 Saturn overtook Jupiter in 2023 when astronomers confirmed its 145th moon. Most are tiny captured asteroids barely a few kilometres wide. Scientists are still finding more.

text: Sharks are older than trees. True or false?
options: False — they appeared at the same time
True — but only by a few thousand years
funFact: 💡 Sharks have existed for about 450 million years. Trees only appeared around 385 million years ago. Sharks survived all five mass extinctions — including the one that wiped out the dinosaurs.

text: What causes seasons on Earth?
funFact: 💡 Earth is actually closest to the Sun in January — northern hemisphere winter. Seasons are caused entirely by the 23.5-degree axial tilt, not by how close Earth is to the Sun.

text: Humans use only 10% of their brains. True or false?
options: False — we use virtually all of it
True — only during sleep do we use more
answer: False — we use virtually all of it
funFact: 💡 Brain scans show activity across almost all regions, even during sleep. The 10% myth likely came from misquoted psychology research in the early 1900s — and it never went away.

text: Which element makes up most of your body by mass?
funFact: 💡 About 65% of your body mass is oxygen — mostly locked in water molecules. Carbon adds another 18%. Despite feeling solid, you're mostly made of the same gases floating in the air around you.

text: What colour is blood inside your veins before it gets oxygen?
funFact: 💡 Blood is always red. Deoxygenated blood is a darker red, not blue. The blue-ish colour of veins you see through skin is an optical illusion — skin absorbs red light and reflects blue, making veins appear blue-tinted.

text: A tomato is scientifically a fruit or vegetable?
funFact: 💡 Botanically, a fruit is any seed-bearing structure grown from a flower — making tomatoes, cucumbers, and avocados all fruits. In 1893, the US Supreme Court ruled tomatoes were a vegetable for trade purposes. Science and law still disagree.

text: Why does your stomach replace its lining every few days?
funFact: 💡 Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve metal. The stomach constantly replaces its mucous lining to avoid digesting itself. When this process fails, the result is a painful stomach ulcer.

text: Sound travels fastest through which material?
funFact: 💡 Sound moves at ~343 m/s through air, ~1,480 m/s through water, and over 5,000 m/s through steel. The denser and more rigid a material, the faster sound vibrations travel through it.

text: What does a virus need to reproduce?
funFact: 💡 Viruses aren't technically alive — they have no metabolism and can't reproduce alone. They survive by hijacking a living cell's own machinery to copy themselves. This is why antivirals are so much harder to make than antibiotics.

text: Which animal can technically reverse its own ageing?
funFact: 💡 Turritopsis dohrnii is only 4.5 mm wide but can revert to its juvenile polyp stage when stressed — essentially resetting its biological clock. Scientists call this 'biological immortality.' It's the only known animal that can do this.

text: What actually causes a rainbow?
funFact: 💡 Each raindrop acts like a tiny prism, bending light so different colours exit at different angles. The Sun always needs to be behind you to see a rainbow — and no two people ever see exactly the same one.

text: Which sense fades most noticeably as humans age?
funFact: 💡 Humans have about 450 types of smell receptors and gradually lose them throughout life. Since smell and taste are tightly linked, a declining nose is also why elderly people often say food has lost its flavour.

text: What is it called when a massive star collapses and light can't escape?
funFact: 💡 The first-ever image of a black hole was captured in 2019. The black hole photographed — in galaxy M87 — is 6.5 billion times the mass of our Sun. The image took a network of eight global telescopes working together to produce.

text: What is Earth's layer order from the outside in?
options: Crust, Mantle, Outer Core, Inner Core
Mantle, Crust, Inner Core, Outer Core
Crust, Outer Core, Mantle, Inner Core
answer: Crust, Mantle, Outer Core, Inner Core
funFact: 💡 Earth's inner core is a solid ball of iron roughly the size of the Moon — and it's hotter than the surface of the Sun at around 5,400°C. It stays solid only because the pressure squeezing it from all sides is absolutely immense.

text: How far is the nearest star beyond our Sun?
funFact: 💡 Even travelling at the speed of light — 300,000 km/s — it would take over 4 years to reach Proxima Centauri. Our fastest spacecraft today would take about 75,000 years to cover that distance.

text: Antibiotics can cure the flu. True or false?
options: False — antibiotics don't work on viruses
True — with a strong enough dose
answer: False — antibiotics don't work on viruses
funFact: 💡 The flu is caused by a virus, and antibiotics only target bacteria. Misusing antibiotics on viral infections is a leading cause of antibiotic resistance — one of the most serious global health threats of our time.

text: Why do astronauts float inside the Space Station?
funFact: 💡 The ISS is constantly falling toward Earth — but it's moving sideways so fast that it keeps missing. That's what an orbit actually is. Gravity at ISS altitude is still about 90% as strong as on Earth's surface.

text: Which happened first in Earth's history?
funFact: 💡 Single-celled microbes appeared in Earth's oceans about 3.7 billion years ago. Dinosaurs didn't arrive until 230 million years ago. Humans only showed up around 300,000 years ago — barely a blink in Earth's timeline.

text: Why does touching ice feel cold?
options: Ice draws heat away from your skin
Ice releases cold energy into your hand
Ice lowers pressure on your nerves
Ice blocks warmth from reaching you
answer: Ice draws heat away from your skin
funFact: 💡 'Cold' isn't a thing — it's the absence of heat. When you touch ice, heat flows from your warmer hand into the colder ice. Your nerves detect heat leaving your skin and interpret that as coldness.

text: What percentage of your DNA do you share with a chimpanzee?
funFact: 💡 You share about 98.7% of your DNA with chimps — our closest living relatives. You also share 85% with mice, 60% with fruit flies, and about 50% with a banana. DNA is surprisingly universal across all life on Earth.

text: Which gas do plants absorb to make food?
funFact: 💡 A single mature tree can absorb around 22 kg of CO₂ per year. The carbon gets locked into the wood, leaves and roots — meaning trees are literally built from the air. When wood burns, that carbon gets released back.

text: What was the first image ever captured of a black hole?
options: Taken in 2019 by a global telescope network
Taken in 1969 during the Moon landing
answer: Taken in 2019 by a global telescope network
funFact: 💡 The Event Horizon Telescope wasn't one telescope — it was eight radio observatories on different continents working simultaneously, creating a virtual telescope the size of the entire Earth.

text: Your body makes a new skeleton roughly every how many years?
funFact: 💡 Bones are constantly being broken down and rebuilt by specialised cells called osteoclasts and osteoblasts. Most of your skeleton is replaced over roughly a decade — meaning the skeleton you have today is not the one you had 10 years ago.

text: Which statement about black holes is FALSE?
funFact: 💡 Black holes emit no light — that's what makes them black. They're detected indirectly, by observing their effect on nearby gas and stars. The famous 2019 image showed glowing material swirling around one, not the hole itself.

text: Which element did NOT exist right after the Big Bang?
funFact: 💡 Only hydrogen, helium, and trace lithium formed in the Big Bang. Every heavier element — including the carbon in your body — was forged inside a star and scattered into space when that star exploded as a supernova. You are literally made of stardust.

text: Which scientist is correctly matched with their discovery?
funFact: 💡 Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 by accident — he noticed mould killing bacteria on a petri dish he was about to throw away. That near-miss became the foundation of modern antibiotics, saving an estimated 200 million lives.

text: Why does ice float on water?
options: Water expands when frozen, reducing density
answer: Water expands when frozen, reducing density
funFact: 💡 Almost every other substance is denser as a solid than as a liquid — water does the opposite. This is essential for life: ice floating on top of lakes insulates the liquid water below, allowing fish and plants to survive winter.

text: What percentage of your DNA do you share with a banana?
funFact: 💡 Both humans and bananas share the same ancient cellular machinery for metabolism and DNA replication that evolved over a billion years ago. You share 98.7% with chimps, 85% with mice, and 60% with bananas — DNA is surprisingly universal.

text: One DNA fact is TRUE — which one?
options: One cell's DNA stretches ~2 metres long
Humans share only 50% DNA with each other
DNA only exists in the nucleus
answer: One cell's DNA stretches ~2 metres long
funFact: 💡 Every single one of your ~37 trillion cells contains about 2 metres of DNA coiled inside it. Stretch all your DNA end to end and it would reach from Earth to Pluto and back — several times over.

text: Which came first in scientific history?
funFact: 💡 Darwin published evolution 7 years before Mendel published genetics — yet Darwin had no idea how traits were inherited. Mendel's work was ignored for 35 years, only rediscovered in 1900, long after both men had died.

text: Roughly how much of the ocean has humanity explored?
funFact: 💡 We have better maps of Mars than of our own ocean floor. Challenger Deep — the deepest point at nearly 11 km — has been visited by fewer people than have walked on the Moon.

text: Why is the night sky dark despite billions of stars?
options: The universe is too young — distant light hasn't arrived
Most stars are too small to see
Stars only emit light toward nearby planets
answer: The universe is too young — distant light hasn't arrived
funFact: 💡 This is Olbers' Paradox. The universe is only 13.8 billion years old, so light from the most distant stars simply hasn't reached us yet. The darkness of the night sky is actually scientific evidence for the Big Bang.

text: What actually causes a sonic boom?
options: A continuous shockwave trailing the aircraft
A single bang when breaking the sound barrier
Sound waves piling up then bursting
answer: A continuous shockwave trailing the aircraft
funFact: 💡 A sonic boom isn't a one-time event — it's a continuous cone-shaped shockwave that follows the plane as long as it's supersonic. People on the ground hear a boom when this cone sweeps over them, not when the barrier is first broken.

text: Which body systems keep working under full anaesthesia?
options: All of them — heart, lungs, digestion
answer: All of them — heart, lungs, digestion
funFact: 💡 The autonomic nervous system runs your heartbeat, breathing, digestion, and hormones completely independently of conscious thought. These systems run 24 hours a day from birth to death without ever needing a deliberate instruction from you.

text: Which is NOT a real element on the periodic table?
funFact: 💡 Oganesson (118), Einsteinium (99), and Moscovium (115) are all real elements. Oganesson is named after physicist Yuri Oganessian — one of only two scientists to have an element named after them while still alive.

text: Why do you feel weightless on a rollercoaster's steepest drop?
options: Your body and seat fall at the same rate
Gravity is briefly cancelled by speed
Centrifugal force pushes you from Earth
Your inner ear temporarily shuts off
answer: Your body and seat fall at the same rate
funFact: 💡 Weightlessness is always caused by free fall — your body and surroundings accelerating together at the same rate. Astronauts feel 'weightless' for exactly the same reason: they and the spacecraft are falling around Earth simultaneously.

text: Which statement about the speed of light is TRUE?
options: Nothing with mass can reach light speed
Light speed is the same in all materials
Light speed was measured in the 20th century
Light travels faster in water than in air
answer: Nothing with mass can reach light speed
funFact: 💡 As a massive object approaches light speed, the energy needed to accelerate it approaches infinity — making it physically impossible. Light actually slows down in water and glass; only in a perfect vacuum does it hit its maximum speed of 299,792 km/s.

text: If the Sun suddenly vanished, when would Earth notice?
funFact: 💡 Both light and gravitational changes travel at the speed of light. If the Sun vanished, Earth would continue orbiting empty space for 8 minutes before both the darkness and the loss of gravity reached us — simultaneously.

text: Why does the Moon have far more craters than Earth?
options: Earth's weather and geology erase craters
answer: Earth's weather and geology erase craters
funFact: 💡 Earth has been struck thousands of times, but erosion, plate tectonics, water, and vegetation continuously resurface the planet. Scientists have confirmed about 190 impact craters on Earth — but estimate hundreds more are buried or eroded away.

text: Lightning never strikes the same place twice — true or false?
options: False — the Empire State Building is struck ~25 times per year
True — static charge dissipates after a strike
False — but only on metal structures
answer: False — the Empire State Building is struck ~25 times per year
funFact: 💡 Tall structures are struck repeatedly because they provide the shortest path between charged clouds and ground. The Empire State Building was once struck 8 times in 24 minutes during a single storm.

text: One fact about sleep is TRUE — which one?
options: Brain is more active in REM than while reading
Body temperature rises during deep sleep
Muscles stay active during REM sleep
answer: Brain is more active in REM than while reading
funFact: 💡 During REM sleep, brain activity is nearly indistinguishable from being awake. Your body is temporarily paralysed during REM to stop you physically acting out dreams — a mechanism called REM atonia. When it fails, people sleepwalk or thrash around.

text: Which animals both use echolocation independently?
funFact: 💡 Bats and dolphins evolved echolocation completely independently of each other — one of the most striking examples of convergent evolution. Some bats can detect an object as thin as a human hair using sound alone.

text: Walk on the Moon with no suit — what kills you first?
funFact: 💡 In a vacuum, dissolved gases in your blood would bubble, your saliva would boil at body temperature, and your lungs would empty instantly. Consciousness fades in about 15 seconds. Radiation and temperature cause serious harm too — but slightly slower.

text: Why does the sky turn red and orange at sunset?
options: More atmosphere scatters blue light away
The Sun cools as it sets
Ozone filters blue light at the horizon
answer: More atmosphere scatters blue light away
funFact: 💡 At sunset, sunlight travels through up to 40 times more atmosphere than at noon. Blue light scatters completely away, leaving only long-wavelength reds and oranges. Ironically, clean air produces less colourful sunsets — pollution and dust actually enhance them.

text: What would happen if you dropped a ball through a tunnel straight through Earth?
options: It oscillates back and forth endlessly
It stops at the other side
It melts before reaching the core
It exits faster than it entered
answer: It oscillates back and forth endlessly
funFact: 💡 In a frictionless vacuum tunnel, the ball would reach the other side in exactly 42 minutes — the same time as a low Earth orbit. This thought experiment inspired real proposals for 'gravity trains' that could travel between cities without engines.

text: Which of these is a common science myth most people believe?
options: The heart stops during a sneeze
funFact: 💡 Humans actually have many more than five senses — including proprioception (body position), thermoception (temperature), nociception (pain), and the vestibular sense (balance). The 'five senses' idea dates back to Aristotle and has never been quite right.

text: Which scientist first proposed that Earth orbits the Sun?
funFact: 💡 Copernicus proposed his heliocentric model in 1543 but delayed publication until he was near death, fearing the Church's reaction. He reportedly received the first printed copy of his book on the very day he died.

text: Why is a sunset more colourful after a volcanic eruption?
options: Ash and sulphur particles scatter light more
CO₂ from eruptions filters blue light
Lava glow reflects off clouds globally
answer: Ash and sulphur particles scatter light more
funFact: 💡 After the 1883 Krakatoa eruption, people worldwide reported vivid red and purple sunsets for over a year. The fine particles ejected into the stratosphere travel around the globe and dramatically enhance light scattering — the same physics as a normal sunset, but amplified.

text: The Great Wall of China was built by one dynasty in one go. True or false?
options: False — it was built across many dynasties over centuries
True — the Qin dynasty built it all
True — but only the eastern sections
False — it was built by a single emperor over 20 years
answer: False — it was built across many dynasties over centuries
funFact: 💡 Construction of the Great Wall started around 7th century BC and continued through the Ming dynasty (1368–1644 AD). Most of what tourists visit today is Ming-era wall. The total length of all sections combined is over 21,000 km — long enough to circle the Earth halfway.

text: Napoleon Bonaparte was famously short. Was this actually true?
options: No — he was around average height for his era
Yes — he was unusually short even for his time
Yes — he was the shortest French emperor ever
No — he was actually above average and very tall
answer: No — he was around average height for his era
funFact: 💡 Napoleon stood around 5'7" — average or above for a Frenchman in the 1800s. The myth largely came from British wartime propaganda mocking him as 'Little Boney'. A unit conversion mix-up between French and English inches also made him appear shorter on paper than he actually was.

text: The Berlin Wall fell in 1989. How did it mostly come down?
options: Crowds tore it apart with hammers and bare hands
Bulldozers demolished it overnight under military order
It was blown up in a controlled explosion
It collapsed on its own from structural damage
answer: Crowds tore it apart with hammers and bare hands
funFact: 💡 On November 9, 1989, East Germany announced citizens could cross freely — and thousands immediately flooded checkpoints. Crowds climbed the wall, danced on top of it, and smashed it with hammers. The pieces became some of the most traded historical souvenirs ever — fragments are still sold today.

text: Which ancient wonder of the world is still standing today?
funFact: 💡 The Great Pyramid is the only one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World that still exists. Built around 2560 BC, it was the tallest man-made structure on Earth for nearly 4,000 years — a record not beaten until the Lincoln Cathedral was completed in England around 1311 AD.

text: Which civilisation built the Colosseum in Rome?
funFact: 💡 The Colosseum was completed around 80 AD and could hold up to 80,000 spectators. It hosted gladiator fights, animal hunts, public executions, and — in its early days — could even be flooded for mock naval battles. Romans invented the concept of entertainment venues on this scale.

text: Which explorer is credited with first arriving in the Americas in 1492?
funFact: 💡 Columbus never actually set foot on mainland North America — he landed on Caribbean islands. The Americas are named after Amerigo Vespucci, who later realised these were entirely new continents, not Asia. Columbus went to his grave believing he had reached Asia.

text: Ancient Egyptians used what material to write on before paper?
funFact: 💡 The word 'paper' literally comes from 'papyrus' — the reed plant that grew along the Nile. Egyptians would slice the reeds into strips, press them together, and dry them into sheets. Some papyrus documents from ancient Egypt have survived over 4,000 years and are still readable today.

text: Which famous structure did the ancient Egyptians build as tombs for their pharaohs?
funFact: 💡 Over 130 pyramids have been discovered in Egypt. The workforce that built them was not slaves — recent evidence shows they were paid workers who received wages, beer, and medical care. Workers who died on the job were even given proper burials near the pyramids they helped construct.

text: Which ancient city was famously destroyed by a volcanic eruption and preserved under ash?
funFact: 💡 Mount Vesuvius erupted in 79 AD and buried Pompeii under 4–6 metres of volcanic ash in under 24 hours. The ash preserved the city so perfectly that archaeologists found entire loaves of bread, intact wall paintings, and the outlines of people frozen in their final moments. It's one of the most complete ancient cities ever found.

text: The first humans to walk on the Moon were from which country?
funFact: 💡 Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin landed on the Moon on July 20, 1969, during the Apollo 11 mission. Armstrong's first words were 'That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.' The Soviet Union had led the Space Race early, launching the first satellite and first human in space — but the US won the Moon race.

text: Which ancient Greek city-state was famous for its fierce warrior culture?
funFact: 💡 Spartan boys were taken from their families at age 7 and entered a brutal military training programme called the agoge. They slept on reed beds, were given minimal food intentionally (to encourage foraging), and trained until age 30. The English word 'spartan' — meaning austere and disciplined — comes directly from them.

text: Which famous ship sank on its maiden voyage in 1912?
funFact: 💡 The Titanic sank in just 2 hours and 40 minutes after hitting an iceberg. It had enough lifeboats for only about half the people on board — a legal minimum at the time. The wreck wasn't discovered until 1985, lying 3,800 metres below the North Atlantic. It has been slowly deteriorating ever since.

text: Ancient Romans used what instead of toilet paper?
options: A shared sponge on a stick
answer: A shared sponge on a stick
funFact: 💡 Romans used a 'xylospongium' — a sea sponge on a wooden stick — shared among public toilet users, rinsed between uses in a vinegar or salt water channel. Roman public toilets had no individual cubicles — they were communal benches. Romans considered going to the toilet a social activity, not a private one.

text: Which invention by Johannes Gutenberg around 1440 changed the world?
funFact: 💡 Before Gutenberg's printing press, books were copied by hand — a single Bible could take a monk over a year. Within 50 years of the press's invention, over 20 million books had been printed across Europe. It directly enabled the Reformation, the Scientific Revolution, and mass literacy — arguably the most impactful invention in human history.

text: Ancient Egyptians worshipped cats. Which statement about this is TRUE?
options: Killing a cat — even accidentally — was punishable by death
Only priests were allowed to own cats
Cats were only sacred in upper Egypt, not the delta
Cats were worshipped but could not enter temples
answer: Killing a cat — even accidentally — was punishable by death
funFact: 💡 Cats were so sacred in ancient Egypt that when a family cat died, the whole household shaved their eyebrows as a sign of mourning. Bastet, the cat goddess, was one of the most popular deities. Entire cat cemeteries have been found — archaeologists have discovered over 300,000 mummified cats in a single ancient site.

text: Which famous structure was built as a tomb and is still one of the world's most visited monuments?
funFact: 💡 The Taj Mahal in Agra, India, was built by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan between 1632 and 1653 as a mausoleum for his wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died in childbirth. Over 20,000 workers and 1,000 elephants were used in its construction. Shah Jahan was later imprisoned by his own son — in a fort where he could only see the Taj Mahal from a window.

text: Which country gifted the Statue of Liberty to the United States?
funFact: 💡 France gifted the Statue of Liberty in 1886 as a symbol of friendship and shared democratic values. The French paid for the statue itself; Americans fundraised for the pedestal. The statue's full name is 'Liberty Enlightening the World'. Inside the torch there is a balcony that has been closed to visitors since 1916.

text: The ancient Olympics were held in honour of which Greek god?
funFact: 💡 The ancient Olympics were held at Olympia every four years starting around 776 BC. Only Greek-speaking free men could compete — women were banned from even watching. Athletes competed completely naked. Wars between Greek city-states were paused during the Games in a truce called the 'Olympic peace'.

text: Which famous scientist was also an artist, engineer and inventor during the Renaissance?
funFact: 💡 Da Vinci designed a working helicopter, solar power, a calculator, and a tank — all in the 1480s, centuries before any were built. He filled over 13,000 pages of notebooks with ideas, observations, and sketches. He painted the Mona Lisa and The Last Supper while simultaneously designing bridges and studying anatomy by dissecting corpses.

text: Which is NOT a real ancient wonder of the world?
funFact: 💡 The Seven Wonders of the Ancient World were a Greek tourist list from around 225 BC. The Leaning Tower of Pisa was completed in the 14th century AD — about 1,500 years too late for the list. Of the original seven, only the Great Pyramid of Giza survives. The Hanging Gardens may never have existed at all.

text: The Vikings were the first Europeans to reach North America. Who led the expedition?
funFact: 💡 Leif Erikson reached North America around 1000 AD — roughly 500 years before Columbus. He called it 'Vinland'.
